topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the faded photograph, the edges softened by time, the smile on her face a vibrant echo of shared secrets. She had painstakingly reconstructed the memory of their shared childhood—the scraped knees, the whispered plans under the old oak tree. Now, sitting across from Liam, who was laughing easily at something entirely unrelated, the silence in the room felt cavernous. When she finally managed to speak, her voice was thin, almost reedy. "Remember that summer by the lake?" she asked, the question hanging in the air like an unanswerable riddle. Liam blinked, a slow, deliberate movement, and then his gaze drifted away, a flicker of confusion crossing his features before he turned back to his friend. Elara felt a strange lightness in her chest, as if someone had just lifted a heavy weight, followed immediately by a profound sense of disorientation. Her hands, resting on the table, began to tremble almost imperceptibly.

[story 2]

I replayed the last few minutes in my head, the sound of my own voice catching in my throat. Liam had just casually mentioned a trip he took last year, a story that involved a distant cousin and a mountain hike. I tried again, softer this time, my usual buoyant energy completely drained. "We built that terrible fort in the woods behind your house, remember?" I watched his expression shift—a momentary furrow of concentration, then a blankness that settled over him like a heavy fog. He shifted in his seat, picking at a loose thread on his jeans, his eyes distant, as if trying to pull a forgotten file from a deep recess of his mind. A tightness constricted my throat. I wanted to argue, to insist that those moments were etched into his very being, but the words wouldn't form. I just watched him, my jaw slackening slightly, a strange, hollow sensation spreading through my limbs.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug suddenly felt cold against my palms. I had rehearsed this conversation a hundred times in my head, building up the courage to bring up the old times, the undeniable bond we’d shared since kindergarten. When I finally launched into the story of the disastrous attempt to bake a cake together, Liam just nodded politely, his attention clearly elsewhere. His eyes, usually so quick and bright, seemed to glaze over for a beat, like a camera focusing on the wrong subject. I leaned forward, my posture rigid, desperate for some sign, any acknowledgment that those years hadn't evaporated for him. The air seemed to thicken around me, pressing down. I opened my mouth, but no sound emerged, just a slow, heavy intake of breath, my entire frame suddenly feeling unsteady, as if the ground beneath my feet had momentarily dissolved.
